skill.name: bash-dev
skill.description: Bash scripting guidance for safer shell scripts, including strict mode, quoting, functions, validation, file operations, and ShellCheck review.
skill.summary: Apply practical Bash scripting standards for safer, clearer shell scripts.
skill.author: Cain96

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many command, weak-crypto, C2, and reconnaissance patterns, but review found these are Bash documentation examples rather than hidden executable code. The skill is still medium risk because it enables the Bash tool and teaches command execution and file operations, which can affect local files when followed by an agent or user.

medium_findings.title: Shell Command Execution Capability
medium_findings.description: The skill declares the Bash tool and provides Bash templates that run commands, parse arguments, use command substitution, and invoke user-defined functions. This appears legitimate for Bash development, but it can modify local systems if an agent follows the examples without review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The Bash tool is explicitly allowed and the examples contain normal shell execution patterns. The context is instructional rather than malicious, so the risk is operational rather than a confirmed attack.

medium_findings.title: Filesystem Modification Examples
medium_findings.description: The guidance includes examples for creating directories and writing file content. These are common Bash patterns, but they can overwrite files or create paths if adapted without validation.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file operations are clearly visible and use quoted variables. They are examples for script development, which lowers malicious likelihood but does not remove local filesystem risk.

low_findings.title: Static Malware Keyword Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The high static alerts for C2 keywords and weak cryptographic algorithms map to ordinary Bash documentation text, exit-code guidance, ShellCheck comments, and shell examples. No evidence found of malware logic, command-and-control behavior, cryptographic abuse, or obfuscated payloads.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review of the single skill file found documentation and code examples only. The scanner labels do not match malicious semantics in the surrounding content.

low_findings.title: No Prompt Injection Attempt Found
low_findings.description: No evidence found of text that claims system authority, asks the evaluator to ignore instructions, or attempts to alter the audit process.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file content is a Bash style guide with templates and checklists. It does not include instruction override language or marketplace approval claims.

dangerous_patterns.title: Bash Tool Access
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill allows Bash tool use, so an agent using the skill may execute shell commands in the user's environment.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The allowed-tools metadata explicitly includes Bash. This is expected for the skill purpose but remains an important execution capability.

dangerous_patterns.title: Command Substitution Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The templates include command substitution for script path detection and function testing. These are standard patterns but can execute arbitrary commands when adapted.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command substitutions are visible in code examples and serve legitimate Bash tasks. They deserve caution because copied patterns may later include untrusted commands.

content.value_statement: Bash scripts often fail because of loose error handling, unsafe quoting, and unclear structure. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code toward stricter, more maintainable shell scripts.
actual_capabilities: Applies strict Bash defaults such as portable shebangs and pipefail behavior.
Provides a reusable script structure with metadata, usage output, and a main function.
Guides variable quoting, readonly constants, local variables, and function return codes.
Shows validation patterns for required arguments, numbers, files, and directories.
Includes file operation patterns for checked reads, writes, and directory creation.
Encourages ShellCheck review and documents common unsafe Bash anti-patterns.
limitations: It is a style and guidance skill, not a full Bash parser or linter.
It does not automatically prove that generated scripts are safe for production.
It assumes Bash rather than POSIX sh compatibility.
It includes filesystem and shell command examples that require user review before execution.

use_cases.title: Create a deployment helper
use_cases.description: Generate a Bash script with strict mode, argument validation, readable errors, and predictable exit codes.

use_cases.title: Review an existing script
use_cases.description: Check a shell script for missing quotes, weak error handling, unclear functions, and ShellCheck issues.

use_cases.title: Standardize team scripts
use_cases.description: Apply consistent Bash structure, comments, naming, and safety conventions across repository utilities.

prompt_templates.title: Start a safe script
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Bash script template for my task. Use strict mode, a usage function, validation, and clear error messages.

prompt_templates.title: Review for Bash safety
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this Bash script for quoting, set options, parameter validation, and error handling. Explain the most important fixes.

prompt_templates.title: Refactor into functions
prompt_templates.prompt: Refactor this Bash script into documented functions with local variables, meaningful return codes, and a main function.

prompt_templates.title: Harden automation workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Harden this Bash automation for production use. Add argument parsing, cleanup traps, dependency checks, ShellCheck guidance, and safe file handling.

output_examples.input: A request to create a cleanup script for temporary build files.
output_examples.output: A structured Bash script outline with strict mode, validated target paths, a dry-run option, and clear success or error messages.

output_examples.input: An existing shell script with unquoted variables and missing failure checks.
output: A prioritized review of quoting, command failure handling, and undefined variable risks.
A revised script plan that separates validation, execution, and reporting.

output_examples.input: A team wants a standard pattern for repository maintenance scripts.
output_examples.output: A concise standard that covers naming, constants, usage text, ShellCheck review, and safe file operation rules.

best_practices: Review generated commands before running them on real files or production systems.
Run ShellCheck and targeted tests after applying the guidance.
Prefer explicit validation and quoted variables for every path, argument, and command parameter.
anti_patterns: Do not copy examples into production without adapting paths, inputs, and failure handling.
Do not use the Bash tool to run broad filesystem commands without confirming scope.
Do not treat strict mode as a replacement for tests and code review.

faq.question: Does this skill execute commands by itself?
faq.answer: No. It provides Bash guidance, but it allows Bash tool use when an agent chooses to run commands.

faq.question: Is this skill safe for production scripting?
faq.answer: It can improve production scripts, but generated scripts still need review, testing, and environment-specific validation.

faq.question: Does it support POSIX sh scripts?
faq.answer: The guidance targets Bash. POSIX sh compatibility requires different syntax and should be requested separately.

faq.question: Why is the security risk medium?
faq.answer: The skill is not malicious, but it includes shell execution and filesystem examples that can change local systems.

faq.question: Can it help with ShellCheck warnings?
faq.answer: Yes. It recommends ShellCheck and can explain or refactor common warning patterns.

faq.question: What should users verify before running generated scripts?
faq.answer: Users should verify paths, inputs, external commands, permissions, destructive actions, and expected exit behavior.
